Headline: Haye your say on park plans
Full Text:
SHAILER Park residents are encouraged to provide feedback to Logan City Council on proposed upgrades to Adelong Park.

    The draft plan is to remove the existing playround equipment and replace it with a large play structure with slides, a climbing net, a swing set with an inclusive basket swing and spring rockers for younger children.

    The existing basketball court will be refreshed with new line marking, a backboard and net.

    New bench seats and footpath connections are also planned, while the park's existing shelters with seating and bin will remain.

    Residents can provide feedback until Tuesday 10 February.

Headline: Bench sparks conversation
Full Text:
A BRIGHT yellow community bench has been installed at Boronia Bushland Reserve to help spread awareness about the impact of domestic and family violence.

    The seat has been installed in remembrance of Brisbane woman Allison Baden-Clay, who was killed by her husband in 2012.

    The orange and yellow bench features sunflowers, which were Ms Baden-Clay's favourite flower, and symbolise kindness and hope.

    Logan Division 7 councillor Tim Frazer said the council hoped the bench would prompt conversations about domestic and family VIO lence in our communities. The bench is one of several installed across Logan to raise awarness of domestic violence.

    Others are at Middle Park, Forest Glen Park, Silvertop Park, Sweetgum Park and outside the Logan West Community Centre.

    The bench was unveiled by Vanessa Fowler from the Allison Baden-Clay Foundation with Cr Frazer and Member for Waterford Shannon Fentiman.

Headline: Grant helps uni students get started
Full Text:
LOGAN City Council has given 10 recent high school graduates $5000 grants to put towards their university study.

    Canterbury College graduate Mitchell Dixon will use his grant to study a combined Bachelor of Engineering/Master of Engineering at the University of Queensland's St Lucia campus.

    "I decided at the start of year 11 that I wanted to do engineering because I liked physics and maths," Mr Dixon said.

    "I ended up having to do biology in Year 12, and that made me realise I want to do something in the health industry.

    "I found out that I could specialise in mechanical and then major in biomedical engineering, to create robotic equipment that will help people."

    Mr Dixon, who lives in New Beith and will turn 18 next month, said he was excited to start his coursework and participate in university life.

    "I'm excited to see my friends and make some new ones and I*1l also partake in some of the ensembles they have there," he said.

    A few of Mr Dixon's friends will also be studying a Bachelor of Engineering at The University of Queensland this year, but may specialise in other areas, he said.

    "I'm going to use the bursary for uni resources like textbooks and stationery, so it will just help me stress less about having to work as much to be able to afford everything "I honestly had no clue what my chances were of getting the bursary, so it was a nice surprise," Mayor Jon Raven said the City of Logan Tertiary Educational Bursaries program helped local students pay for resources like textbooks and technology.

    "When you walk out of the school gates for the last time, there's a lot of uncertainty about what the future might bring," Cr Raven said.

    "We're proud to get behind those who've enrolled in a degree with some financial support.

    "It's important they start out on the right foot with things like textbooks and technology, so they can focus on what really matters."

Caption Text:
Councillor Paul Jackson with Mitchell Dixon and bursary sponsors.

Canterbury College graduate Mitchell Dixon will use his grant to study a combined Bachelor of Engineering/Master of Engineering at the University of Queensland's St Lucia campus.

Headline: Angry parents call to pause development
Full Text:
RESIDENTS are fed up roads and infrastrucure they say aren't adequate to handle development, with school zones being some of the worst for traffic jams.

    New Beith resident Bonnie MacRae already has 1400 signatures on a petition to "pause development" in her area, in a bid to reach the "heart of the decision makers" to create a better plan for traffic flow.

    "Our communities are growing rapidly, but the infrastructure hasn't kept pace," she said.

    "We are witnessing an influx of new hous ing estates, yet the planning, transport and education departments haven't addressed the critical needs of existing residents," "This petition is intended to raise concern with the local and state bodies that approve the developments in the immediate region, to the community impact and risks associated with such large-scale developments, relating to safety and infrastructure demand."

    Ms MacRae said school zones were some of the worst areas affected with drivers taking unnecessary and dangerous risks to pass traffic jams.

    "The ever-expanding traffic and insufficient public transport options have left our roads congested and our children stranded, sometimes waiting for buses that simply cant make it through the traffic on time," 9: she said.

    "Without immediate action to improve and expand the roadways, the safety of our chil dren and all residents is at risk.

    Ms MacRae said a pause on development would allow time for a comprehensive review of existing plans.

    "This pause will give us time to develop robust infrastructure, including expanding public transport systems and road networks, aligned with the needs of our growing communities," she said.

    Mayor Jon Raven said the Queensland Government, through the Department of Education, was responsible for managing school safety.

    "We know that planning for schools lags growth, which puts pressure on roads that were never designed for that kind of facility and often means there is never enough parking for parents," he said.

    "As part of council's city-wide Parking Enforcement Plan 2023-2026, council aims to help make school drop-off and pick-up times smoother and safer for local families through education and enforcement of parking regulation."

    Jimboomba mum Beck Capuzzo said all levels of government needed to stop playing the blame game.

    "They keep building and approving all these new estates but there are no new roads to get around, and every new house has a minimum of two to three cars, so what used to be 100 cars on the road has double or tripled," she said.

    "Something needs to be done." MyCityLogan contacted The Department of Education for comment.

    Mayor Jon Raven said "funding constraints limit what and when council can deliver" when it comes to new infrastructure.

    "We're the fastest growing city in the state, with around 12,000 new residents choosing to move to Logan every year," he said "We're also home to two Queensland Government Priority Development Areas at Yar rabilba and Flagstone, which are exclusively approved and managed by Economic Development Queensland."

    Cr Raven said the council was obliged to support the State's priority areas with out-ofsequence infrastructure.

    "Building this infrastructure means we have less funding available to support the growth in areas like Park Ridge and Logan Reserve which also want to see improvements to their road networks, he said.

    He said this was an "example of why the council should be solely responsible for planning development across the entire city," Ms MacRae's 'Change' petition has received over 1400 signatures and she hopes government will listen.

    Transport and Main Roads, and the Department of Education were contacted for comment but had not responded at the time of publication.

    Comment at mycitylogan.com.au

New Beith resident Bonnie MacRae already has 1400 signatures on a petition to "pause development" in her area, in a bid to reach the "heart of the decision makers" to create a better plan for traffic flow.

Headline: 4000 submission prompt flood plans
Full Text:
JON RAVEN, MAYOR OF LOGAN ONE of council's major projects this year is a review of flood risk policy matters as part of the city's draft new planning scheme, Logan Plan.

    Council is continuing to review the more than 4000 submissions received during community consultation on the draft Logan Plan in September and October 2025.

    To make it easier for you to see how our work is progressing we've set up a webpage outlining where we started, where we're up to, and what's next.

    There's an option to subscribe for email updates so you can be notified each time there's a change.

    We've also outlined our community communication plan for the next 12 months beThe 11 November 2025 edition of MyCity Logan, talking about council's flood mapping crisis.

    cause we want to be transparent at every step of the way.

    This webpage is all about making relevant information more accessible.

    You can view the information at logan.gld.

    gov au/planning-and-bulding/ilocd-maps Based on your feedback so far, we've also included some frequently asked questions, including about insurance, which I know is a key issue for many people.

    Last week I visited Suncorp for meetings about the insurance price increases residents had sent through to me.

    They're looking into all of these and will contact every single customer that has sent through their unusually high premiums.

    Another thing we are working on is the way information is presented in the Logan Flood Portal and in our property flood reports.

    Our Community Reference Panel recently met to look at some options at the end of last month and offered some really interesting insights. Once we've worked through that feedback, we will launch a city-wide Have Your Say survey in March so everyone can give their feedback on the Logan Flood Portal and flood reports.

    All of these changes will take some time, but I'Il make sure the community can stay informed and involved throughout the process.

Headline: Out now – grab your free ‘Our Logan' magazine for summer!
Full Text:
More than $80,000 in arts funds for Logan creatives  Local creative artists are receiving more than $80,000 from the city of Logan's Regional

   Arts Development Fund (RADF) for projects ranging from visual arts, dance, film and a Tik Tok series.

    

    The fund, provided by the Queensland Government and Logan City Council, supports the development of arts and cultural practices including visual arts, craft and design, multimedia, film, theatre, music, dance, literature and writing.

    

    Mayor Jon Raven says the diversity of projects reflects the city's creative scene.

    

    'Every year we see a huge amount of creativity in the applications and this round is a great example of that,' Mayor Raven says.

    

    'From children's books to feature film scripts and even a TikTok dark comedy series, the sky's the limit when it comes to what can be achieved in Logan.

    

    'This financial year, Council has provided more than $69,550 to 14 recipients who can launch their works to national and international audiences.'

    

    Minister for Education and the Arts John-Paul Langbroek says RADF has an impressive track record of supporting local arts and cultural programs across Queensland communities.

    

    'The Crisafulli Government's multi-year RADF investment provides greater funding certainty for 59 regional councils, streamlined program management and red tape reduction, while boosting the state's vibrant arts and cultural scene,' Minister Langbroek says.

    

    'For 34 years, Arts Queensland has partnered with regional councils to co-invest in local arts initiatives that generate jobs, community engagement and help boost local economies. The Crisafulli Government will continue to support these vital collaborations.'

    

    A total of $83,871 has been awarded in this round of RADF funding to 14 recipients from Logan:

    

    Andy Siong ($5,000) for the exhibition Unboxed: Giving A Voice To Wildlife! This exhibition, to be held at Logan Art Gallery from 30 October to 5 December 2026, uses childlike cardboard sculptures and an artist's book to unbox wildlife voices and encourage the audience to take small actions to protect local wildlife.

    

    Sally Terare ($4,902) to create a catalogue to accompany the Inner Dreaming, Spirit and Healing exhibition to be held at Logan Art Gallery from 10 April to 13 June 2026.

    

    Chona Allen ($4,995) to create Canvas in Motion – Avant-Garde Fashion Show and Art Exhibition , an interdisciplinary project that fuses art, fashion, and sustainability. The exhibition is at Logan Art Gallery from 9 December 2026 to 16 January 2027.

    

    Suzanne Webcke ($9,994) to create Serial Killer Anonymous , a 14-episode dark comedy web series developed for Tik Tok and Instagram to be released mid-2026.

    

    Nestor Lucas ($3,000) to develop a feature-length film based on a story about enduring a mid-life crisis set against the 2011 floods.

    

    Kailani D'Amour ($5,000) to write and develop Secret Family Recipe , a thriller/crime feature film.

    

    Bel Thomson ($5,000) to write and publish a children's book.

    

    Rebecca McIntosh ($10,000) for the exhibition Flying Fox Woman – The Woman's Safety project , at Logan Art Gallery from 9 December 2025 to 16 January 2027.

    

    Vanghoua Anthony Vue ($10,000) delivering The Great Kerbside Nation Building Project , with research, workshops, and exhibitions exploring kerbside collection as a cultural practice shaped by migration and community.

    

    Beenleigh Theatre Group ($9,991) to develop, produce and stage Flight! A New Musical , a new musical celebrating misremembered and forgotten figures from aviation history. Logan artists are partnering with Ipswich artists and creative organisations to support the development and production of this original musical at Beenleigh Theatre Group in October 2026.

    

    Eliza Adam ($4,996) for Holding Force – Exhibition and Workshops , responding to the Logan region's histories and landscapes. These works will be exhibited at the Logan Art Gallery from 31 October to 5 December 2026 and accompanied by a public program of workshops.

    

    Greg Huglin ($4,313) for The Portmanteau of Possibilities: A Multimedia Film and Performance Project . Led by Academy Award-winning cinematographer Greg Huglin, the project engages Logan's youth and emerging artists in a multimedia film to-stage project transforming digital storytelling into a live cinematic performance.

    

    Jenine Olliver ($4,970) for Behind the Signs , a paid writer's workshop and writing period to produce 6 draft scripts for an online comedy documentary series.

    

    Australian Dance Council Ausdance (QLD) Inc ($1,170) for Safedance for Kids Workshop/Open Day , delivering practical professional development for dance teachers in Logan.

    

    For more information and to apply for the next round of funding, visit logan.qld.gov.au/radf

    

    PHOTO AT TOP: RADF recipient, visual artist Andy Siong.

Headline: Family torn apart in horrific fatal crash
Full Text:
A little girl will grow up without her mother and grandfather after they were killed in a triple-fatal crash south of Brisbane as the family returned home from a day out.

    Jessica Ortiz, 33, and her father-in-law Jorge Ortiz, 65, died on Saturday when their Volkswagen station wagon was struck head-on by a Toyota HiLux at Yarrabilba. Jessica's daughter two-year-old Sylvie Ortiz, her husband Damian Ortiz and mother-inlaw Patricia Ortiz were also in the car and suffered serious injuries. They remain in hospital.

    The 18-year-old driver of the Toyota HiLux, Izayiah Aitken, also died after his car rolled on Waterford Tamborine Rd and caught fire.

    Jessica and Damian welcomed their first child Sylvie in 2023, and got married in August 2024.

    They were returning home to Brisbane's north from a day out with Damian's parents, Jorge and Patricia Ortiz, when they were hit by the Toyota at high-speed.

    Friend Russell Rackley set up a fundraiser for the Ortiz family in the wake of the tragedy.

    "We are coming together as friends and family to lift up the Ortiz family during an unimaginable loss," Mr Rackley posted.

    "A tragic car accident took the lives of Jorge and Jess, leaving hearts broken and lives forever changed.

    "As the family begins to face the days ahead, the road to healing will be long and incredibly difficult.

    "We ask for your love, prayers, and support as we stand beside them and help carry them through this heart breaking time."

    Jessica was a nurse at Brisbane Private Hospital who had worked there for about seven years. One of her colleagues shared a tribute to social media, saying work would not be the same without her there.

    Tributes had been laid at the crash site on Monday Forensic Crash Unit is investigating the crash and is appealing for anyone with dashcam footage or information to contact police.

Headline: Man has date with Beauy Magistrate
Full Text:
A 22-year-old Yarrabilba man has been remanded in custody to face Beaudesert Magistrates Court on drugs, weapons and stolen property charges.

    According to police, on January 24, Logan District intelligence officers, in conjunction with Logan District Tactical Crime Squad, acted on information received about a man who was allegedly in possession of multiple illicit firearms and ammunition, and was allegedly attempting to sell the items through online platforms.

    Logan District Tactical Crime Squad searched a Springbrook Street address in Yarrabilba on 25 January, finding eight firearms, including two that had been cut down and were concealable, along with a quantity of cocaine, cash, and other drug paraphernalia.

    The man was not at the address at the time of the search but was soon arrested and charged with 22 offences. He will face Beaudesert Magistrates Court on 24 March.

    TRIPLE FATALITY Three people dead in a traffic crash at Yarrabilba on 7 February.

    The accident happened about 4.30pm when a white Toyota Hilux utility reportedly collided with a white Volkswagen station wagon on Waterford Tamborine Road.

    Police said the Toyota Hilux caught fire as a result of the collision.

    The driver and sole occupant of the Hilux, an 18-year-old Marsden man, died at the scene.

    The driver of the Volkswagen, a 65-year-old Stafford Heights man, and one of the passengers, a 33-year-old Kedron woman, also died.

    Two adult occupants of the Volkswagen were taken to the Princess Alexandra Hospital with critical injuries. A 2-year-old girl, also travelling in the Volkswagen, was taken to Queensland Children's Hospital in a serious but stable condition.

    No further information was available at time of publication.

Headline: Two charged after death of baby boy
Full Text:
A CLEVELAND man, 25, has been charged with murder and a 24-year-old Southport woman has been charged with cruelty to chil dren following the death of a three-week-old boy.

    Police allege the baby boy died from fatal internal injuries sustained at a property in Rosemary Street at Greenbank.

    The baby was taken to Queensland Children's Hospital in a critical condition on 18 July 2024.

    Police said he had no obvious injuries and died in hospital four days later.

    Police said detectives from the Logan Child Protection and Investigation Unit and Child Trauma Unit began a long and thorough investigation into how the boy died.

    The Cleveland man was expected to appear at the Brisbane Magistrates Court on Monday, while the woman is expected to appear at Beenleigh Magistrates Court on 4 March.

Headline: Pilot school to help at risk youth
Full Text:
A YOUTH justice school designed to lower youth crime rates by helping improve outcomes for high-risk teenagers will be built at Logan Central, the State Government announced this week.

    Ohana Academy at Logan Central will be the first youth justice school of its kind in Queensland and is expected to begin enrolling students who have been referred to the school in the second half of 2026.

    Ohana for Youth founder Aaron Devine, whose non-profit organisation will run the school, said he hoped to deliver positive outcomes for both young people attending the school and the broader community.

    "This pilot represents a practical investment in long-term change for young people and communities," Mr Devine said.

    "When young people are supported to re-engage in education, build skills and reconnect with positive pathways, the benefits flow through to families, communities and community safety," Mr Devine said.

    Ohana Academy will deliver a specialised behavioral reform program to re-engage young people with education, employment and the community.

    Individual case management, one-on-one mentoring, family support and parental coaching will be available for Ohana students.

    Places will be offered for students aged 12 to 17 who have been issued police cautions, may be completing community service orders or are currently on bail.

    Students will be supervised by school staff for up to 12 hours per day, five days a week, and will work to a 'project-based' curriculum with specialist teachers and extracurricular activities.

    Ohana for Youth will run a second youth justice school in Cairns Minister for Youth Justice and Victim Support Laura Gerber said the schools were part of the State Government's $40 million plan to build dedicated youth justice schools and divert young people from crime.

    Queensland Premier David Crisafulli said Youth Justice Schools were a critical step to making Queensland safer and reducing vic tims of crime.

    He said victim numbers were down 7.2 per cent in 2025 compared with the previous year, based on population.

    "With stronger laws, more police, early intervention and rehabilitation we will start to restore safety," Mr Cristafulli said.
Summary: A YOUTH justice school designed to lower youth crime rates by helping improve outcomes for high-risk teenagers will be built at Logan Central, the State Government announced this week. Ohana Academy at Logan Central will be the first youth justice school of its kind in Queensland and is expected to begin enrolling students who have been referred to the school in the second half of 2026.

Headline: Women's issues take top priority
Full Text:
A WOMEN who fled family and domestic violence and then built a charity to help other women escape will be the keynote speaker at this year's City Women Logan forum in March.

    Dandelion Safe Homes director Karen Muras built her own tiny home after fleeing a domestic violence relationship and earning qualifications in building design.

    Now she helps other women design and build tiny homes to give families a safe and affordable space when they need it.

    In partnership with The Twin Rivers Centre, Dandelion Safe Homes has already trained 20 women in Certificate 1 Construction, with 18 of them gaining employment.

    The women gain experience in building tiny homes as part of their training.

    A tiny home the women built for emergency accommodation was featured at the Tiny Homes Expo at the Cleveland Show Grounds in October.

    They are currently working on two more; a single level tiny home and a donated modular building to be fittied out as a long term home.

    Impact Care Service, a non-profit community service branch of Impact Church, is hosting the forum for Logan women from a range of backgrounds to respond to issues such as family and domestic violence, homelessness and community connection. The City Women Logan forum will be held from 10am to 4pm on Saturday 14 March at Impact Church at Slacks Creek.

Headline: Ballroom's best sparkle
Full Text:
THERE will be sparkly heels. sequins and plenty of pizzazz when Australia's best ballroom dancers head to Logan to compete at the Crystal Classic Championship in March.

    About 400 dancers are expected to showcase their talent at the event, which is run by the Queensland Branch of the Australian Dancing Society.

    Organiser Linette McFarlane said the event would be held at the Logan Entertainment Centre for the first time this year, after being held at the Logan Metro Sports Centre for the past four years.

    "We hold competitions throughout the year, but this championship is bigger and better," Ms McFarlane said.

    "People travel from all over Australia to come and compete in this ballroom dancing championship.

    "I have a studio based in Ipswich, so my students will be there and, of course, they re from all over too."

    Last year, Ms McFarlane's son Ryan Zimmerman and his partner Evita Vasiliauskaite placed sixth in the world at the Blackpool Dance Festival, which is an internationally acclaimed annual ballroom dancing competition.

    For the Crystal Classic, danc ers will compete in one of the championship's age divisions, which will see performances from kids aged nine and under in the sub-juvenile category all the way through to adults aged 60 and over in the masters three category.

    Ms McFarlane said the judges would be looking for craft and presentation when scoring a danc er's performance.

    "We're looking at footwork, we're looking at grooming, we're looking at posture, we're looking at how they hold themselves, their top lines, their rhythmical value, and whether they can hear the music," she said.

    "It's all prestigious and very glamorous."

    Not every dancer competing is seasoned, Ms McFarlane said.

    For some, it will be their first time taking the stage.

    "It caters to all different standards of dancing, from elite to very beginner."

    Tickets will be sold at the door and people are welcome to attend anytime from 8am to 10pm.

    "We're just trying to give back to our local area and give Logan something really exciting to come watch and sit back and enjoy," Ms McFarlane said.

    "It is a truly beautiful sport," she said.

    The Crystal Classic Championship will be be held at the Logan Entertainment Centre from 8am on Saturday 7 March.

Headline: Cans boost local coffers
Full Text:
MORE than 100 million containers have been recycled at Jimboomba's Containers for Change location, generating $10 million for local recyclers.

    The Exchange Shed owner Megan Lever said she was proud of the impact container refunds lodged by local families, community members, charities, groups and schools have had on the community.

    At 10c a container, $10 million has been earned from community recycling efforts at The Exchange Shed, since it opened in 2019.

    "Seeing how container refunds help us support local causes is pretty amazing it shows the real power of recycling," Mrs Lever said.

    "It's a privilege to give back to the people and groups who support us."

    In 2021, The Exchange Shed partnered with Rosies Friends on the Street, which supports people experiencing homelessness.

    It donates an estimated $20,000 a year to support Rosies fundraising initiatives.

    Locals can also drop off essential non-perishable food items such as instant cup noodles, biscuits and cordial at The Exchange Shed, which Rosies Friends on the Street distributes across south-east Queensland.

    Rosies _ Friends on the Street head of fundraising Lacee Hennessy Overton said The Exchange enabled her organisation to provide support to the community's most vulnerable.

    "Every action that Megan and her team take enable us to provide comfort, connection, friendship and referral to people experiencing homelessness, disadvantage and social isolation," Mrs Hennessy Overton said.

    "Our volunteers could not do the work they do every night without the support of generous individuals and business owners like Megan. 939 Container Exchange interim chief executive officer Trevor Evans, whose organisation runs Containers for Change, congratulated the Jimboomba depot for its impressive achievement.

    This milestone reflects the extraordinary difference a single depot can make when it becomes part of the heartbeat of its community," Mr Evans said.

Headline: Rates rise will not dampen demand for Logan homes
Full Text:
LOCAL real estate agents say the increase in mortgage repayment interest rates will have little to no effect on Logan's property market.

    The Reserve Bank of Australia raised interest rates by 0.25% on Tuesday 3 February, due to lingering economic inflation.

    It was the first rate rise since 2023 and the cash rate now sits at 3.85%.

    LJ Hooker estate agent Pragya Ojha said housing stock in Springwood was tight, and with few properties currently on the market, buyer demand was expected to continue.

    "I think such a small increase will not have a substantial impact on buying and selling activity," Ms Ojha said.

    Mortgage holders are likely feeling nervous this increase could be the first of a few, Ms Ojha said.

    But overall, she said she thought the rate hike would have little impact on the market.

    "If rate rises continue and people have mortgages on more than one property, then they could be forced to sell," she said.

    "But for others, 0.25% won't have a substantial impact, people will still try to hold on."" For mortgage holders of around $600,000, re-payments will increase by $100 a month as a result of the rates hike, according to figures published in the Brisbane Times.

    All major banks, including the Commonwealth Bank of Australia. Westpac Banking Corporation, National Australia Bank, and Australia and New Zealand Banking Group Limited, have adjusted their interest rates to align with the Reserve Bank's rate rise.

    Ray White principal Avi Khan agrees rate rises are unlikely to cause any major shifts in Logan's property market.

    "Rate cuts don't matter because it [Logan] is still the most affordable place for first home buyers and investors in the greater Brisbane area," Mr Khan said.

    "And we're seeing strong entry-level demand from changes to state and federal government policy that is driving price growth even further."
Summary: LOCAL real estate agents say the increase in mortgage repayment interest rates will have little to no effect on Logan's property market. The Reserve Bank of Australia raised interest rates by 0.25% on Tuesday 3 February, due to lingering economic inflation.

Headline:
Interview
Lynne, who's clearly battling with the traffic, says, Steve, memo to Tom Sharp, it probably takes less time to fly to Rockhampton than to drive down the M1 to the scenic rim, says Lynne, wit
Full Text:
Interview
Lynne, who's clearly battling with the traffic, says, Steve, memo to Tom Sharp, it probably takes less time to fly to Rockhampton than to drive down the M1 to the scenic rim, says Lynne, with several exclamation marks. Lynne, thank you. On 6.12am. Streaming live on the ABC Listen app. 6.12, ABC Radio Brisbane. With Steve Austin. Just while on roads and some of the dramas that you're facing in a moment, as you've heard, the Waterford Tambourine Road Southern Logan has claimed four lives in three days. Transport Minister Brent Mickelberg spoke to our breakfast duo, Craig and Loretta, this morning, where they asked the Minister if the increase in accidents was driver behaviour or road conditions. This is the Transport Minister's reply. Well, the short answer, Craig, is it's both. And as I said, all of us who use the roads, and like most Queenslanders, I use the roads every single day, and I too see that sort of behaviour. I think what we need to do is we need to shift behaviours. Obviously, police will be out enforcing the laws, and our focus as a government is on ensuring that we free up the police so they're more able to be out and about enforcing with that proactive whether it's road safety or other community policing concerns. And we'll build the built infrastructure as well. Things like the Residential Activation Fund, $2 billion, that's all about addressing some of the concerns concerns that were just articulated by your corner. We call it building the infrastructure required to support the population growth that we are going to receive here in Queensland, whether we like it or not. We're going to see that population growth. People want to move to Queensland, so we need to build the infrastructure. And what we've seen is probably 10 years at least where we haven't had the infrastructure built to keep up with that population growth. Do we need, like, heavy rail through that Yarrabilba corridor, for instance? Well, absolutely. An investment in heavy rail is part of the solution. So it's why we're building Logan and Gold Coast Faster Rail. A massive investment in increasing the frequency and the reliability of rail to the Gold Coast.
Yeah, but people still need to get in their car from Yarrabilba to then get to one of those train stations.
Yeah, absolutely. And it's why we're also boosting boosting bus services. So recently we rolled out $70 million, which is to fund boosted bus services in exactly the kind of places we're talking about. And just last week or the week before, I think we talked about rolling out more bus services in Logan as well, in addition to the Ipswich area. So, you know, these issues are complex and they require a response that addresses both public transport, driver behaviour and built infrastructure in terms of the roads. But I guess the message to your listeners is it's incumbent on all of us it's not just of government, it's a job for every single road user to drive to the conditions and to get home safely. And ultimately, when I think about road safety, I think about the impact on on those who are left behind, the families who are left behind to pick up the pieces after these tragic incidents.
Transport Minister Brent Mickelberg, if you travel the Waterford-Tambourine Road, I would dearly love to know how you find it when you travel it, particularly if you do it every day in your commute. 1300 222 612 is the phone number. Or send a text 0467 922 612.

Minister Mickelberg emphasized that improving road safety is a shared responsibility between the government, involving police enforcement and infrastructure upgrades, and all road users.
story_title: Triple-Fatal Yarrabilba Crash Sparks Road Safety Debate
story_summary: A devastating head-on collision on Waterford Tamborine Road in Yarrabilba claimed three lives: Jessica Ortiz (33), her father-in-law Jorge Ortiz (65), and teenager Izayiah Aitken (18). Several other family members, including Jessica's two-year-old daughter, were seriously injured. In response to recent fatal crashes in the area, Transport Minister Brent Mickelberg acknowledged long-term underinvestment in road infrastructure and driver behaviour as contributing factors. The Queensland Government has outlined several major infrastructure projects, including a $2 billion Residential Activation Fund and faster rail for Logan, to address the issues. Police are investigating the crash and have appealed for dashcam footage.

Headline: Youth justice school headed to Logan Central
Full Text:
A youth justice school, designed to improve outcomes for high-risk teenagers and lower youth crime rates, will be built in Logan Central, the state government announced this week.

    Ohana Academy in Logan Central will be the first youth justice school of its kind in Queensland and is expected to begin enrolling students who have been referred to the school in the second half of 2026.

    

    Founder of Ohana for Youth, the non-profit youth organisation that will run the school, Aaron Devine, said he hoped to deliver positive outcomes for young people attending the school and the broader community.

    

    "This pilot represents a practical investment in long-term change for young people and communities," Mr Devine said.

    

    "When young people are supported to re-engage in education, build skills, and reconnect with positive pathways, the benefits flow through to families, communities and community safety."

    

    The school, Ohana Academy, is expected to deliver a specialised behavioral reform program to re-engage young people with education, employment and the community.

    

    Individual case management, one-on-one mentoring, family support and parental coaching will be available for Ohana students, age 12 to 17, who have been issued police cautions, may be completing community service orders, or are currently on bail.

    

    Day-to-day students will be supervised by school staff for 12 hours per day, five days a week, and work to a 'project-based' curriculum.

    

    A second youth justice school, delivered by the same Gold Coast-based non-profit youth charity, Ohana for Youth, will be built in Cairns.

    

    Both schools are part of the state government's $40 million investment to build dedicated youth justice schools, which they hope will have a knock-on effect on youth crime numbers, Minister for Youth Justice and Victim Support Laura Gerber said.

    

    "These schools will offer a specialised education curriculum in a structured, disciplined environment for high-risk teenagers on youth justice orders to divert them from crime," Minister Gerber said.
Summary: A youth justice school, designed to improve outcomes for high-risk teenagers and lower youth crime rates, will be built in Logan Central, the state government announced this week. Ohana Academy in Logan Central will be the first youth justice school of it...

There is no mention of Logan City Council, any of its councillors (including Mayor-elect Jon Raven), or any of its official publications or media bulletins.

While it doesn't cover other specific local issues like infrastructure or housing, its focus on a new youth justice school aimed at reducing crime and improving safety makes it relevant to the 'community safety' and 'law enforcement' themes within the Logan context.

auto_summary: The Queensland state government will build Ohana Academy, a pioneering youth justice school in Logan Central, as part of a $40 million investment to reduce youth crime. Operated by the non-profit Ohana for Youth, founded by Aaron Devine, the school will open in late 2026 to provide a specialized reform program for high-risk teenagers aged 12-17 who are involved with the justice system. Minister for Youth Justice Laura Gerber stated the school, along with a second planned in Cairns, aims to divert youth from crime through education, mentoring, and family support in a structured environment.
story_title: State's First Youth Justice School to Open in Logan
story_summary: The Queensland Government has announced a $40 million investment to establish Ohana Academy in Logan Central, the state's first dedicated youth justice school. Set to open in late 2026, the school will be operated by the non-profit Ohana for Youth, founded by Aaron Devine. It will provide a specialized reform program for high-risk teenagers aged 12-17 who are involved with the justice system. The initiative aims to re-engage youth through education, mentoring, individual case management, and family support to divert them from crime and improve community safety.

Headline:
There's not because we've been down this road. So it's the places where you have the most activity, which is food production. And so what we're going to do...
And least diversified economies too.
And
Full Text:
There's not because we've been down this road. So it's the places where you have the most activity, which is food production. And so what we're going to do...
And least diversified economies too.
And we're going to pay people. And I don't know, is this going to be offshore income, the new carbon money, or is it going to be taxpayers' dollars paying farmers not to grow food, not to process the food in the cities, and certainly not to eat it?
So I can't answer the, you know, the purpose of the study was kind of like what we talked about in previous estimates which is taking that question of, well, if we were to sequester 119 million tonnes of carbon in the landscape, what would it look like?
The mechanisms that will drive the demand side are a separate kind of question and some of that comes through the safeguard and all those other demand that ACCU uses as the country looks to decarbonise.
So we put that question aside because we haven't looked at that aspect of the the problem. I think that one of the key kind of positives out of this is that food production is not really at risk. Yes, we do have an impact, but the impact is quite modest.
I don't see how, Dr Granville, because it's the most productive areas.
But it's a small amount of the most productive areas.
No,
I had 8 million hectares.
Australia doesn't have that much productive land. Like, it's about 4 or 5... Not even. 3 or 4% 4% per cent of Australia is actually productive for cropping and for intensive agriculture. And that's a bit you've just taken out a really significant portion of.
And these are all the little farms that grow the food that we all eat. Australia's a food bowl for the rest of the country.
When you say it's modest, I mean if I were to propose a 2% reduction in all public service salaries to fix the budget, what do you reckon? Would that be seen as modest?
Sounds good.
Probably not.
I can imagine their reaction. But in the sense that it's also offset by additional revenue as well.
You and I both remember when they introduced paid parking in Belconnen That was going to be a riot.
We saw some maladaptive behaviour as well at that time. Let's do this comparison. It's a $2.8 billion hit on your sums. I think live sheep exports is around $50 million or something of exports a year. So this is 60 or... Is that right?
I'd have to check.
It's about 60 times that. Because anybody can make these comparisons. It's only modest because All right, well, okay.
Look at everything we're having to do to shut down the live sheep industry and all the adjustment packages we need.
And here we've got an impact that's many multiples of that, many multiples of that, and there's zero assistance or adjustment being provided to these rural communities that will be affected.
I do not want to downplay the idea that they are trade-offs, because we are all aware that they are trade-offs. We also know that the agriculture sector is going to to grow. Incomes and returns in rural areas and so forth are still going to grow. We're expecting productivity growth to continue and farm profitability will, you know, in large part rest on how future future productivity goes. So can I just question that assumption given that the costs of production, fuel, wages, insurance, land taxes are all increasing and we've just done less vegetation management opportunities.
You know, government is leaning into farm productivity at at every corner and yet you think profitability is going to grow.
That's not what farmers are saying. But I think we're on solid ground with that. So we are still seeing productivity growth in the agriculture sector and while I acknowledge that productivity growth is slow.
I think the Senator said profitability though, didn't you? Yeah, I'll come back.
But also profitability is growing. So farm profitability has grown over the last 20 years about 3%. We've had a pretty good terms of trade, though. That's the risk. With terms of trade and also with... The sector is outperforming the market sector in terms of productivity growth, hands down. And so there are a number of positive things, despite, you know, I don't disagree that there's challenges in the sector and there's these other changes and so forth. So I think it's not an unreasonable assumption to think that the sector could continue in what it's doing in terms of overcoming those challenges and experiencing some growth. We've got these significant investments we were talking about earlier in our R&D system which are paying off and so forth. So there are positives in that sense. I don't think that's an unreasonable assumption to expect that the sector will continue to grow and profitability will continue to improve or at least be maintained which is effectively where we're looking at and in that sense you know we can talk about you know big or small modest or whatever but I think it does lay out that there are there is an opportunity space here we can acknowledge that there's an opportunity cost but in terms of the opportunity for some of those areas that adds a diversification element to a farming system which as we discussed doesn't really exist and as markets become more variable and we're dealing with geopolitical structures and like a diversification option is a good thing for landholders and for farmers and in other areas it will change the production mix around and we will get some balanced economy impacts.
I feel like I'm sorry.
I feel like we live in two different worlds. So in Queensland, 40% of farmers growing fruit and vegetables have said they want to exit the market because of supermarket supermarket monopolies, price pressures, both costs going up and prices from monopolistic practises from supermarkets pushing prices down.
These are in the areas with the high rainfall and the most productive lands, and yet we're proposing that this will be a terrific idea that we should plant some trees, give them a subsidy, and they should just grow less. This will be a massive impact and it means that coals and woolwares continue to buy their fruit and veggies from offshore so Australians won't even notice until the boats stop or there's some problem and we have a problem with food security. So just to qualify, so we've looked at the impacts on broadacre land, so we haven't looked into the impacts on horticultural production. So this is a broadacre story, so cattle and cropping and sheep. And so that impact as well is on that. amount of foregone agricultural activity is foregone broadacre activity, not on horticultural and so forth. So given what the question was... So it'll be fine for the veggie farmers? So the fruit and vegetable farmers and those are separate to, I think, the pressures that we're talking about and the opportunity cost and the lost production in this space that we've modelled. I was just going to add to my colleague's comments, Senator Canavan, and just to kind of, I guess, again, encourage a broader view of this as well. We've talked about the challenging impacts of drought on farmers in this committee today as well. And I guess I acknowledge that the diversification of income streams that this work points to also provides farmers with an extra stream of income which supports their resilience during difficult times. I guess we are both concerned with productivity in the sector, but we are also concerned with resilience. I have just got to correct the record. Mr Rick Wilson, the member for Econa, a very diligent member of parliament, he's been watching intently. He does correct me that live sheep accounted for $120 million, I believe. So we're talking about impact 20 times, just over 20 times, the shutdown of the live sheep industry. So it's very significant. And again, there is no, zero assistance being offered by this government for this impact on rural communities. Can I check, have you got any plans to... Well, what is assistance? Please enlighten me. Senator McCarthy, please enlighten me on the assistance you're providing to Castler and Cody. You just asked the Minister and she's answering and you started shouting over. Chair, I did not have a question. You just called her on. She interrupted me. She started answering. Maybe you should listen, Chair. She interrupted me. I'm listening. So, Minister, you were going to say something. No, she interrupted me. I had the call. I was just going to say, Chair, I was just going to say, before I was rudely interjected on, I was just going to say that we would never tell the farmers what to do with their land.
No, Senator McCarthy, you said that my claim that there was zero assistance is not true. So what assistance are you providing the Tenterfield Council to adjust to a 5% to 10% reduction in agricultural production in that area?
Well, I would be more than happy to take that question on notice.
But you just said you are providing assistance.
I jumped in to say you said our government is doing nothing. I jumped in to say that we would never, ever tell farmers what to do with their land.
So that is my statement and I'm very happy to take your question on notice. I'll just ask you to give you another chance. Maybe your advisor's been in the back of the room there.
You obviously don't know what you're talking about. So maybe your advisors could inform you. I'll ask again, Minister. Sorry, Minister.

So we're talking about impact 20 times, just over 20 times, the shutdown of the live sheep industry. So it's very significant. And again, there is no, zero assistance being offered by this government for this impact on rural communities. Can I check, have you got any plans to... Well, what is assistance? Please enlighten me. Senator McCarthy, please enlighten me on the assistance you're providing to Castler and Cody. You just asked the Minister and she's answering and you started shouting over. Chair, I did not have a question. You just called her on. She interrupted me. She started answering. Maybe you should listen, Chair. She interrupted me. I'm listening. So, Minister, you were going to say something. No, she interrupted me. I had the call. I was just going to say, Chair, I was just going to say, before I was rudely interjected on, I was just going to say that we would never tell the farmers what to do with their land.
No, Senator McCarthy, you said that my claim that there was zero assistance is not true. So what assistance are you providing the Tenterfield Council to adjust to a 5% to 10% reduction in agricultural production in that area?
Well, I would be more than happy to take that question on notice.
But you just said you are providing assistance.
I jumped in to say you said our government is doing nothing. I jumped in to say that we would never, ever tell farmers what to do with their land.
So that is my statement and I'm very happy to take your question on notice. I'll just ask you to give you another chance. Maybe your advisor's been in the back of the room there.
You obviously don't know what you're talking about. So maybe your advisors could inform you. I'll ask again, Minister. Sorry, Minister.

auto_summary: The article details a parliamentary committee discussion concerning the impact of large-scale carbon sequestration initiatives, such as planting trees on agricultural land, on Australia's farming sector. Key stakeholders, including Senator Matt Canavan and officials from the Department of Agriculture, debated the economic consequences, with Senator Canavan arguing the plan would cause a multi-billion dollar reduction in agricultural production and disproportionately affect rural communities without adequate adjustment assistance. The departmental representatives acknowledged trade-offs but contended the sector's overall growth and profitability trends, coupled with diversification benefits for farmers, would mitigate the impact, which they characterized as 'modest' and focused on broadacre farming rather than horticulture.
story_title: Debate Over Carbon Sequestration's Impact on Farming
story_summary: A parliamentary committee is examining the economic consequences of large-scale carbon sequestration initiatives on Australia's agricultural sector. Senator Matt Canavan argued that planting trees on agricultural land would lead to a multi-billion dollar reduction in production and harm rural communities. Officials from the Department of Agriculture countered that the impact would be 'modest' and focused on broadacre farming. They contended that the sector's overall growth trends, combined with diversification benefits for farmers, would mitigate any negative trade-offs.

Headline:
Interview
Flags on full display as they step up to the podium. What a proud moment it is. And it's fun to look at the foreign flags and spot the weird ones. Did you know the flag of Nepal is the only
Full Text:
Interview
Flags on full display as they step up to the podium. What a proud moment it is. And it's fun to look at the foreign flags and spot the weird ones. Did you know the flag of Nepal is the only national flag in the world that is not rectangular. Let's hear it for Kathmandu. The Dominican Republic has has a purple parrot. I'm not kidding. You can Google it. Mozambique has a Kalashnikov assault rifle, something about an independent struggle. You can giggle, but have you had a look at your flag? Hey? Which flag? The Brisbane City flag. Do you know your own flag? Do you? This is what is on it. I'm not kidding. Blue and yellow wavy lines, pretzels, snakes and stars. Very busy flag. This is Radius. I'm going to take the time to explain it to you. There are six sections. Three horizontally, two vertically. Very busy. Each of the six sections has one of two designs. Either blue and white wavy lines with a winged snake that's pole dancing. I'm not kidding. A pole dancing snake over wavy lines. The other design is blue background with two pretzels and a star. Seriously, two pretzels and a star. You can do better than this. Let's fix it. it. What should be on the flag? a big sun, a big pineapple, the Story Bridge, the tv towers at Mount Cooth. It's got to be Brisbane. A bearded dragon is a bin chicken. Too trashy. Can you fix it? Yes, you can. 1300 222 612. Call me, don't text. 612 ABC Radio Brisbane. Traffic. Hey, two bigger crash at North Tivoli this afternoon. Just in, Warragah Highway eastbound left lane after Colo Road. Traffic's back to Pine Mountain Road. Couple of breakdowns, one at Belmont Gateway northbound left lane before Old Cleveland Road. And a secondary breakdown as well in that same area in the shoulder as well. So some minor delays through there. Crews get those two breakdowns cleared. We had some rubber matting debris scattered on the Gateway Northbound lanes before Bicentennial Road at Nudgee. That's all being cleared. Has been busy around Nudgee, the South Zoon, and also the Gateway Northbound building between Boondall and the Bruce Highway board hills. Does clear around rum downs on the Bruce Highway. So busy through to there. Centenary Highway southbound is busy into Springfield Lakes, inbound between Sumner and Jindalee. And the Western Freeway, that's been building from Indooroopilly through to Victory Pocket. Sango Road, a pretty good run. Just a little bit of a stop start around the Toomble Road merge at Virginia and Gibby Road between Castledine and Aspley and Bald Hills, getting on to the Bruce Highway. I'm Brad from ATN. That's the latest. More details at half past three and 6.12 ABC Brisbane.
Thanks so much for that, Brad. There's a lot of OMGs on the text line, people Googling the Brisbane flag. I'm not kidding, right? I'm not kidding. This is the most extraordinary thing you have ever seen. Who came up with this jolly thing? Pole dancing winged snakes over wiggly kind of Greek-style white and yellow background. I forgot to mention there's a kind of... ..there's a kind of yellow border or frill round it, like your grandmother's tablecloth as well. And as for the pretzels and the stars, quite bizarre. Vanessa on the text line, Ellen, I'm with you. There needs to be an intervention into this Brisbane flag. We might even need to rally for a Royal Commission Actually, no, nothing will happen if a Royal Commissioner gets involved. That's Vanessa at Mount Crosby, alarmed with some sensible suggestions. I need your input. 1-300-222-612 1-300-222-612 612. What can be done with this flag? I've never seen a pole dancing winged snake in Brisbane before and I've spent most of my 58 years in this city. Taking your calls next. 1300 222 612, 1300 222 612. Warm afternoon, Wavell Heights 30 degrees, no pole dancing snakes there. There's cane toads, you could, cane toads at Cleveland, maybe 30 degrees there. Eagleby 31, Ipswich 33 degrees, Donnybrook 29, Pacificines, 28. Kenilworth this afternoon up Sunshine Coast way, 27 degrees. 1-300-222-612. You're going to need to fix it. It's all well and good to laugh at the flags that are taking up the flagpole for the Winter Olympics. You know, the Nepalese with their triangular flag. It's fairly amusing. The Dominican Republic with their purple parrot. None of those people have pretzels. 11 minutes past three. You think I'm exaggerating. Kelly, you had a look. You know I'm not exaggerating, right? Yeah, that's different.
I'm going pretzels. It definitely looks like pretzels to me, a first look.
Yeah, so there's six sections, as I say, and three of them, two at the bottom and one in the middle at the top, have a pretzel and a star and then a pretzel sort of stacked on on top of one another.
Yes. I'm embarrassed to say I've never seen that before and I didn't even know it existed, so I googled it once you mentioned it and I was like, oh my goodness, it's a pirate flag.
It does have elements of that, doesn't it? Do you agree that the description of the other section as a pole dancing winged snake is pretty much it? Yeah, that reminds me of the medical symbol. Yes, at the chemist or something.
Yeah, so, and I read the description, which you would have seen in my text, which I thought, yeah, I don't know. It's confusing. It's too confusing. Yeah. I'm not sure where they are going to use this.
Well, it's flying above the Brisbane City Hall right now. This is your flag. It's there every day. It's just that we've all got a little bit more interested in looking at a flagpole. What would you put on it instead?
I like blue of our.....that colour blue that we have on the Australian flag, but honestly, I guess I'm thinking of the Queensland flag. When I imagine the Brisbane flag...
I just want a big pineapple in the middle of it. Everybody would look at it and go, yep, that's Vegas. BrisVegas pineapple.
Yes!
That blue blue they've got with the pineapple.
I'll vote for that.
Okay, one vote for blue with a pineapple. Thank you to Kelly affirming that we are not exaggerating here. This is not an exaggeration. You need to Google it if you don't believe it. This is some shocking stuff. Pretzels and snakes and stars and yellow wavy lines. Georgina Robinson was sent out, ABC radio reporter, delving into the history of the flag. You probably probably rolled your eyes at this assignment, Georgina. What was your first reaction upon seeing this extremely busy flag?
My reaction was, you're joking. I have lived in Brisbane basically my entire life, worked with ABC for two years, and I had zero clue that we had a flag and that it looked like that. Yeah. it was a lot to take in.
It is a lot to take in.
And your eye is not drawn to any particular part of There's no focal point for the flag. No, it's divided into six and your eyes are just kind of lost. Very confusing. So many elements and and usually a flag you want it to be like you can see the symbols, they're recognisable but this one you're just like, whew, boy, okay. Kingsley's at Logan this afternoon. I wonder if Logan's got a flag. What do you think of the Brisbane flag, Kingsley? Um, sorry, about the Brisbane flag, I think it's out of date. Whoever did it years ago, it's not relevant, and it doesn't say what Brisbane does. We grow stuff and I think in my part of the world we're pretty good on flags because we've given our flag to 26 other countries around the world. You're talking about the Union Union Jack here. Yep,
There is a view in some quarters that you could probably take it back.
Sorry?
Well, some people would like the top left of their flag back. But anyway, I digress. What would look better for the Brisbane flag, do you think, Kingsley?
I think leaning on what Brisbane is good at, which, well, we've got the river, and I think, yeah, the Story Bridge really should be there.
Brisbane River should be there.
And, yeah, maybe some wildlife as well. I mean... What are you thinking? Koala, bearded lizard, bin chicken? Oh, definitely the bin chicken, just as a bit of tongue-in-cheek, just to have a bit of a laugh.
Yeah, and watch the kids try to draw it in the primary schools. Good thought, Kingsley. On the text line, Matt says they're not pretzels, they're Stafford knots. Now, Georgina Robinson has been tasked with figuring out what the heck with this flag. Are the pretzels Stafford knots?
They are Stafford Knotts and they're kind of a reference to Sir Thomas Brisbane who city was named after. He was part of the Staffordshire Regiment. And so those are the Stafford knots in reference to that.

Headline:
Interview
almost feel like we're Smooth FM all of a sudden. That's Debbie Gibson and Lost In Your Eyes on 612 ABC We'd love to have your company this morning. If you haven't heard the news, the ABC Ne
Full Text:
Interview
almost feel like we're Smooth FM all of a sudden. That's Debbie Gibson and Lost In Your Eyes on 612 ABC We'd love to have your company this morning. If you haven't heard the news, the ABC News team is reporting that there's a quality control problem with the Winter Olympic medals. Winter Olympics organisers... are urgently looking into the apparently flimsy Milano Cortina medals after at least four athletes had their awards fall apart in the first three days of competition. The poor things. Andrea Franceschi, the Chief Operations Officer for the Organising Committee, said it was working on a solution, quote-unquote. Apparently, she says, we are aware of the situation, we have seen the images, obviously we are trying to understand in detail if there is a problem. He goes on to say, obviously, we are paying maximum attention this matter as the medal is the dream of the athletes. So we want that, obviously, in the moment they are given it, that everything is absolutely perfect because we really consider it to be the most important moment. So we are working on it. So apparently there's a quality control problem with the medals for the Winter Olympics in Milano Cortina. This will mean that when you see them bite into their medal, you know, when they're on the podium... On 6.12am and on the ABC Listener. 6.12 ABC Radio Brisbane. Mornings with Steve Austin. Be careful when you bite that gold medal. The Paris 2024 Olympic had an issue with the tarnishing due to a defective protective varnish. Apparently over 100 athletes reported damage, particularly on bronze following the Games and had the medals replaced. I'll move on. How many streetlights are out on your way home? Because they've been damaged by theft of copper cabling. This is one of those problems that just seems to keep on coming here in South East Queensland. Alex from Bar Scrub put this question to Transport Minister Brent Mickelberg on our breakfast programme this morning with Craig and Loretta. Have a listen to this. Minister, you've spoken before about the theft of copper and the outage of streetlights. My partner has complained about coming home late at night and very dark on the Logan Motorway and the Pacific Highway and the roundabouts that don't have lighting due to theft of copper. Of the lighting that's available we mapped our route and there is over 50% of the installed lighting doesn't work because of these thefts. Can you tell me what's going to be done about this because this is now 15 months later?
Minister? Yeah great question, Alex. And you're right, it's a really significant challenge for us and we're seeing increasing copper theft in places like Logan but also in other parts of the network, particularly in the north side of Brisbane, in and around Caboolture, we see significant theft of copper as well. We're looking at lots of different solutions to try to address that. Part of that is ensuring that we put in place greater penalties for those who choose to break the law and destroy public infrastructure. But also it's about looking at different, more innovative solutions. And I think I spoke with Steve Austin a couple of weeks ago about looking at things like replacing copper with aluminium, which is less attractive for those who seek to steal it to sell for scrap. So those sorts of solutions. It's also about making it harder for people to do the wrong So building stronger, more resilient infrastructure. But it's a really difficult challenge because we see it increasingly right across the state.
Alex says they've been in the dark for 15 months. Minister, when's it going to be fixed?
Well, we're prioritising prioritising those areas with greater safety risk. And in particular, intersections are of the greatest concern. But I do acknowledge that the areas, particularly in and around the Logan Motorway, the M1, have both been impacted very heavily. And unfortunately, what we frequently see, and it's incredibly disappointing, is when we do go in and rectify these faults, the same individuals sometimes go back and destroy them again for their own selfish interests.
But are we talking about another 12 months, two years? You're not giving a timeframe. So can you narrow it down for us? Well, as I said, we'll prioritise those areas with the greatest safety risk and we'll continue to tackle this problem. As a government, my job is to ensure we put in place the policy settings and the resources so that we can tackle this scourge. Working really closely with the Attorney-General to see what measures we can put in place to discourage those who want to do the wrong thing as well.
Brent Mickelberg on breakfast with Craig and Loretta on the theft of copper cabling from many of the roads and the lights that you have to deal with. G'day, it's Craig and Loretta here.
We want to take you to

ABC News. We're going to talk to you about how it was that a government MP crossed the floor. You'll have heard that story in the news just now. It's quite the decision for a government MP t
Full Text:
Interview
ABC News. We're going to talk to you about how it was that a government MP crossed the floor. You'll have heard that story in the news just now. It's quite the decision for a government MP to break ranks and do that. Does it mean we're about to have another debate around abortion in Queensland? And stay five, we want your complaints. department, call us. 1300 222 612. 1300 222 612. Because you're going to have the opportunity to talk to experts from the Office of Fair Trading. Is your car a lemon? Are you having trouble getting a refund? They're also going to talk about the bond clean and how to get your money back. Taking your calls as well. It's help without the hassle. Coming up after 5, 1300 222 612. Ring up. The experts from the Office of Fair Trading can answer your questions. 612 ABC Radio Brisbane. Traffic.
Well, peak delays on the majors this afternoon. Only a couple of incidents which is good news. There's been minimal incidents this afternoon. Unfortunately, a two-vehicle crash at North Tivoli, Warrigal Highway eastbound left lane still blocked after Coal Eye Road, traffic solid back to Pine Mountain Road. A breakdown m1 southbound shoulder after Foxville Road at Coomera, there has been some minor delays there. In fact, the m1, a really great run towards the Gold Coast, just a little bit under speed between Underwood and Daisy Hill and northbound around Slacks Creek. Busy on Sanford Road, outbounds through Enoggera, Gaythorne and Mitchelton, also Wardell Street, South Brom Road, that's been heavy between Enoggera and Evander Park. Gympie Road is heavy through Astley, Castledine. Castledine to Bald Hills. Sandgate Road has been a little bit heavy around Virginia, around the Turnbull Road merge and Boondall to Deagon And the gateway northbound from Nudgee through the Bruce Highway, Maramba Downs. And southbound, much heavier now on the gateway between Murray and Belmont. Also delays around the city, Riverside Expressway, Pacific Motorway outbound towards Greenslopes and delays both ways on the Capitacle Bridge this afternoon. I'm Brad from atn, more details at half past four on 612 ABC Brisbane. Thanks so much for that Brad. Eight minutes past four this afternoon. It is 30 degrees right now in Tennyson. It's quite a warm afternoon. Thornlands also 30 degrees. Flagstone 31. Caralee 33 degrees. at Dacobin on the Gold Coast 28 degrees. Mooloolaba on the Sunshine Coast 29 degrees. Possible showers tonight. Bit of spitting rain earlier in the day. Doesn't look like a lot of rain coming your way this afternoon. State Parliament is back for the year, the first meeting for the class of 2026. Alex Brewster, ABC state political reporter, was there. We were hearing in the news, your report, Alex, that government mp breaking ranks and crossing crossing the floor in an effort to reverse the LNP's gag on even discussing abortion. Tell us about that.
Yeah, Alan, this an extraordinary step. So first-time member for Mackay, Nigel Dalton, crossed the floor to vote for a motion moved by the kap to overturn overturn the ban on debate on abortion in Queensland Parliament. Now, you might remember that the lnp was really dogged by this issue in its 2024 election campaign and as a result brought in this ban shortly after coming to government that it would not debate anything to do with abortion on the floor of Parliament. So Mr. Golden said that he'd made this deeply personal decision to join the crossbench and Labor in voting to overturn this ban. He didn't say whether he'd spoken to his party party or the Premier but the Premier did say that Ms Dullin had spoken to him on Monday and he had done this move without the Premier's blessing.
So, Robbie Catter who'll be on 612 ABC Radio Drive tomorrow, Polly's Without the Waffle is the segment after five o'clock, all he was doing at this stage was actually trying to overturn the gag on even discussing the abortion laws. I take it the government easily put that down despite Mr Dalton crossing the floor?
That's right. The government had the numbers, so there wasn't actually any debate even on the motion. There was simply a vote, which did cause little bit of commotion. The Clerk of the Parliament and the Speaker did have to have a rather lengthy conversation to figure out exactly how to proceed, but eventually it was put down. And again, there was still no debate on abortion in Queensland Parliament today.
As the government would prefer it, Alex Brewster with you, reporting from State Parliament. Alex, the Premier and the Police Minister announced the response to the William Biller shooting inquest recommendation. So after that terrible event in which the police officers were killed, the coroner made a series of recommendations. What were they and how has the government reacted?
Yes, so the government will introduce mandatory health reporting for patients deemed a high risk of committing violent gun crime in response to this shooting. So any health professional in the public sector who believes a patient shouldn't have a gun or a firearm because of their physical and mental health will have to contact police. However, clinicians can already report concerns to police, it's just not mandatory. For example, Queensland Health made more than 550 disclosures regarding health practises last year and and the mandatory reporting will not extend to the private health system. So the coronial inquest did recommend that there was a review into the feasibility of mandatory across-the-board mental health checks for weapons licence applicants. The government has rejected that, and the opposition says that it's deliberately ignoring those recommendations from the inquest.
Alex, that means, I take it, that the onus is on the professional carer, the mental health professional, to flag the issue with police. I'm worried that this person might commit violence with a weapon. The onus is not on the police to make a mandatory mental health check when someone applies to own a weapon.
That's right, Ellen. So the clinician will now have that mandatory requirement on them. But. I've already said, they're already empowered to do that. And they are so in the private sector as well. This just goes a slight step further in making it mandatory.
Okay. And they have to imagine that this person might commit violence with a weapon. Presumably, they also have to know that they have access to a weapon.
Yes, and it would also require the health professional to kind of understand a bit more about the person's situation as well, I suppose, to actually know if they are a firearm owner or the likes of that. So it does put a fair bit more responsibility on those clinicians.
Yeah. And, of course, once that recommendation is made, once that reference, that referral is made to police, the police commissioner can then prohibit the person in question from owning a weapon for up to 10 years. There's also been changes to the electoral law being debated, specifically whether or not it's a good idea to lift the ban on property developers making donations. What's happened with that today?
So that's being debated at the moment, Ellen. We are expecting this legislation to either pass tomorrow or early tomorrow. So this was a practise that was brought in 2018 under then Premier Annastacia Palaszczuk which stopped property developers from making political donations in elections. Now, the LNP have long said that this is a political gerrymander, that it creates an uneven playing field because they don't get these donations, which would typically come from property developers, and the Labor Party often have these donations, which come from the union movement, which has a rather large war chest behind them. However, the CCC has, the corruption watchdog, has noted during this legislation that lifting this ban could increase the risk of corruption, particularly in the run-up to the 2032 Olympic and Paralympic Games.
Yeah. Alex, you're not an art critic. You're the political reporter, but you're going to have to wear both hats because you've gone and had a look at the official portrait of former Queensland Premier Annastacia Palaszczuk Do your best to describe it and give us a bit of a review. Yes. So this is the former Premier's official portrait. It was unveiled at Queensland Parliament last night and the former Premier was joined by a cast of Queensland political figures. It was painted by a Brisbane artist, Bronwyn Hill, and it shows Ms Palaszczuk smiling. She's sitting in the successful bid for the 2032 Games. As you said, I'm not an art critic, but in Ms Palaszczuk's own words, she says that it captures the essence of her.
There you go. I wonder about those Olympic rings, what they're going to look like as we get closer and closer to the Olympics, if we're struggling to get ready for them, putting their brand of the Olympics on Annastacia Palaszczuk Good on you. Thanks for your work, Alex. Busy day. Thanks, Ellen. Alex Brewster, ABC parliamentary reporter, state political reporter with those updates there. Dire, predictions for the growth of the Australian economy. Is your standard of living about to plummet? Find out next.

The Queensland government announced a new mandatory reporting requirement for public health clinicians to flag patients deemed a high risk of committing violent gun crime to police, a response to the William Biller shooting inquest, while rejecting broader mandatory mental health checks for weapons licence applicants. Concurrently, the official portrait of former Premier Annastacia Palaszczuk, featuring Olympic rings, was unveiled.

Headline:
Interview
I wonder if there's any complaints about the traffic. 612 ABC Radio Brisbane. Traffic.
Well, it's been a pretty good run on the m1 between Brisbane and the Gold Coast. A really excellent run
Full Text:
Interview
I wonder if there's any complaints about the traffic. 612 ABC Radio Brisbane. Traffic.
Well, it's been a pretty good run on the m1 between Brisbane and the Gold Coast. A really excellent run, actually, to start soon. And the m1 southbound is easing. It was back to eight-mile plains at one stage. We did see a bit of late build-up there at one stage, but just a little bit of under-speed around Springwood now and northbound around Logan Home and Saks There is a breakdown in one southbound shoulder up the Fox Road at Coomera. Has been a little bit of a stop start between Helensvale and Gavin as well. We've got the Brea Drew Vale, Logan Motorway, Westbound before the Gateway Motorway extension and got a breakdown now. Unfortunately the Bruce Highway is not a great run southbound with this breakdown at Beerburham. It's in the right lane before Steve Irwin Way and those delays are well and truly past Johnson Road so very heavy through there. I'm Brad from atn, more detail before six on 612 ABC Brisbane.
Thanks so much for that Brad. Not too many complaints about the roads. Jackie's at Wankerley. What do you want to know about real estate advertising? Ask the Office of Fair Trading. Hi there. There's a house for sale near me and it has sold recently. It has one garage but in the advertising it states one garage and one open air space. Like an open garage, just like a port-a-go. And it's not actually a space for a garage, but all the photos on the real estate site show it as a car space. And on the bottom of the ad, it states the disclaimer that the buyer should do their own research regarding the property aspects.
Yeah, I think that's a fairly standard disclaimer. But what she's saying there, Chris McKenzie, is it's, you know, there's actually not the garages shown in the ad. Is that an issue? Yeah, and I suppose the conundrum that occurs is that the real estate agent is acting on behalf of the seller, so there's a contractual arrangement between them, they must act in their best interest, but without misrepresenting anything to the public.
So, like, yeah, a car space versus a portico versus a double garage, it's a fairly clear delineation And, you know, if you're interested in buying it, you'd be doing your due through your solicitor to ensure that those sorts of things are council approved and the like. But we often do get that and we do look at those to make sure there hasn't been a misrepresentation. So I'm happy for Jackie to show me so I can have a look at the past sale.
And you do keep an eye out for that sort of thing on the off chance that it is this type type of issue where someone's misrepresenting what you're buying. Correct, yeah. Simon's at Red Bank Plains. Another vehicle question.
Good afternoon, gentlemen. I did speak to you about a month or so ago. I will before Christmas, and I followed your advice about my son's car that's just gone into limp mode, etc.
