Headline: Traditional side-riding skills in demand across SEQ
Full Text:
Australian Side Saddle enthusiasts at Versedale Scrub are planning a massive year, with the side saddle discipline increasing in popularity as interest in traditional horsemanship grows.

    The group's founder Simone Johnston and her mother Sandie proudly rode into the equestrian ring at the Stanthorpe Show, which ran from 30 January to 1 February.

    "About a year ago, we were thrilled to be approached by the Stanthorpe Show to run a special side saddle program as part of their horse competition at their special 150th event," Johnston said.

    "The Australian Side Saddle Community was more than happy to accommodate this request and proudly sponsor the program which boasted a five class program for competitors. This formed a special part of the show's 150th year celebrations."

    Sandie Johnson made her debut ride in her brand new Syd Hill side saddle, an Australianmade saddle produced just up the road in Brisbane.

    "Syd Hill is renowned for crafting exceptional saddles, and this particular side saddle is especially unique, having had only one previous owner," Johnston said.

    "Adding to its significance, Syd Hill produced only a limited number of side saddles during the late 1970s and into the 1980s. As a result, these saddles are highly sought after and, due to their popularity and rarity, rarely come up for sale."

    Simone Johnson rode her young paint gelding, Millstream Maverick, who she said was still "learning the ropes" of being a riding horse and, of course, a "ladies' mount" for side saddle.

    "We were also invited to present a side saddle display for the public," she said.

    "This living history demonstration attracted incredible interest from people of all ages and backgrounds, showcasing the elegance, tradition, and historical significance of side saddle riding."

    Johnston said local rider Candace Saunders was unable to participate due to a broken leg, and youth rider Stevie Speechley was also sidelined after undergoing recent leg surgery.

    "Despite this, Stevie played a vital role as part of the ground crew, assisting and supporting her fellow Beaudesert riders throughout the day," she said.

    "We look forward to having our local riders back in the saddle and representing our community at future events both locally and further afield."

    With growing interest from other show societies and a big event planned for later this year.

    Johnson said the first ever ASSC side saddle clinic on 27 June would feature side saddle rider, coach, and advocate, Maggie Pritchard McAllister from the USA.

    "Maggie has been doing this for more than 40 years and we've also got another side saddle instructor, Linda Klein who is going to come over and assist Maggie as well," she said.

    "We've nearly filled out all the spots already because we've had interest from interstate.

    "Wendy Tidbold, my mentor up in Brisbane, had a gentleman called Roger Philpot from the

    UK come over in 2008 to her place to run a small side saddle clinic but other than that, nothing like this has ever been done here, so I think that's why it's drawn quite a bit of interest.

    "In the US and UK they have this sort of thing all the time but it has never happened here."

    The ASSC side saddle clinic will be held at Bellara Park Equestrian Centre, a covered arena at Veresdale.

    For more information visit aussiesidesaddle. com or the Australian Side Saddle Community Facebook page.

Caption Text:
Mother and daughter Sandie and Simone Johnson at the Stanthorpe Show. (Hoofprintz Photography )
Sandie Johnson making her debut in a Syd Hill side saddle at the 150th Stanthorpe Show. (Hoofprintz Photography)
Simone Johnson demonstrates the elegance of riding aside at the 2026 Stanthorpe Show. (Hoofprintz Photography)
Summary: Australian Side Saddle enthusiasts at Versedale Scrub are planning a massive year, with the side saddle discipline increasing in popularity as interest in traditional horsemanship grows. The group's founder Simone Johnston and her mother Sandie proudly rode into the equestrian ring at the Stanthorpe Show, which ran from 30 January to 1 February.

other_news_reason: The article focuses on side saddle equestrian events, community activities, and upcoming clinics, primarily in Stanthorpe and Veresdale. Brisbane. Have your say.

Good morning. Yeah, it's changed dramatically in the 12 or 15 years that I've lived out here. The building housing estates in Whamurram, Woodford, Kilcoy, Blackbuck, Tlingeroy, Namango, all accessed by the Dagula Highway. I know it can be annoying, but I assume that when you're pulling a caravan, you're a bit more relaxed, you're not time-pressured like people in the vehicles behind you. Steve, you can hardly see the course. They've managed to grow trees right across the Spectator finish area. The course is that dangerous to get boats down to. There's viable alternatives in Queensland. And we had one years ago that was going to be Lara Pinter that was championed by a local guy in Queensland, here in Brisbane. That went by the by. And there's another viable opportunity out at Leslie Harrison Dam at Tarragindi, which is 17km from the new proposed stadium in Victoria Park. It's only two or three kilometres from the Olympic. Olympic shooting range. It's already got the infrastructure out there to be able to double-lane highways right past the place. There's multiple So to get into. The government already owns the land. It's controlled by Seqwater So why didn't they choose that, Neville? I'd suggest that different people have got different agendas, that's all, and they don't want to consider it. This was brought to their attention by a local surveyor here in Brisbane, Kevin Holt, whose son is a rower and is on the Australian.....on the Australian... Rowing squad...rowowing squad. Has been for quite some time. Kevin and I I put this up to various people and it just falls in a hole.
Nobody wants to consider it.

story_title: Debate Continues Over 2032 Olympic Rowing Venue
story_summary: The location for the 2032 Olympic Games rowing events remains a point of contention among Queensland stakeholders. The Mayor of the Scenic Rim, Tom Sharp, is strongly advocating for Wairralong Dam, claiming overwhelming support from the rowing community. Concurrently, Sports Minister Tim Mander is set to provide an update on progress for Rockhampton's Fitzroy River as a potential venue. Meanwhile, some residents have criticised the proposed Wyalung Dam venue, arguing that more viable and cost-effective alternatives like the Leslie Harrison Dam have been ignored by the government.

Headline: The cost of being progressive: how interest rate rises affect the bush in 2026
Full Text:
How interest rate spikes affect Australian agriculture  At the Wellshot Hotel in Ilfracombe, publican Tracy Hatch sees the ripple effect firsthand. Picture by Sally Gall.

    While urban mortgage holders are tightening their belts in the wake of the Reserve Bank of Australia's February rate hike, primary producers are faced with a more complex calculation. Just as many have spent the past few years expanding into western Queensland's downs country, there is a concern that the latest interest rate hike could be a direct threat to the viability of a progressive business. As Longreach stock and station agent Richard Simpson put it, "the days of owing a hundred dollars are long gone". "[If] The first [rise] doesn't worry them, the second one will," he said. "These days we owe a body of money - we owe substantial figures, and a small move in interest rates adds up very quickly." He said it was the most progressive people who had gone out on a limb. With cattle and sheep sold on a cents per kilogram basis, even a 0.5 per cent shift in interest can quickly erase the profit margins of an entire season, if the season dried off, turning a progressive move into a high-stakes gamble against the bank. "So we're very fortunate that we've had a good start and we're producing kilos of protein," Mr Simpson said. While the immediate sting is felt in the interest bill, the secondary effects of a rising cash rate create a double whammy for rural producers, according to John Cole, pro vice-chancellor at the University of Southern Queensland in Toowoomba. He points out that higher interest rates often lead to a stronger Australian dollar. That makes Australian beef and grain more expensive for international buyers, effectively lowering the price at the farm gate just as the cost of production rises, making Australian agricultural exports less competitive against other countries. Beyond that is the issue of the public purse. "Interest rates must make the federal government tighten its spending," Professor Cole said. "Because they won't stop spending on key urban constituencies, it means fewer dollars for infrastructure - roads, bridges, communications and the like and that hits rural folks particularly. "So in short, not good." The economic tightening is already shifting the mood in some places. Longreach finance specialist Brecken Curtis notes that the sentiment changed very quickly after a period where many expected rates to peak or even fall. For those who scaled up recently, the focus has shifted abruptly from expansion to survival-based risk management. The founder of Seasoned Finance said the rate rises would be an added cost to doing business in the bush, adding to people's costs of production. He also cautioned that in this cycle, with inflation statistics the way they were, more interest rate rises might be on the cards. "People should have a look at their risk management strategies, perhaps have a portion fixed," he said. "It will depend on individual appetites." The pressure isn't just hitting the primary producers; it's trickling down into the small towns that service them. At the Wellshot Hotel in Ilfracombe, publican Tracy Hatch sees the ripple effect firsthand. While the liveability of the outback remains a major draw, discretionary spending is the first thing to go when the bank comes calling, she said. "There comes a time where you just can't keep absorbing costs," Ms Hatch said, noting that for regional hospitality, the margin for error was becoming razor-thin. Her family has a North Queensland sugarcane farming background and she said interest rates were why they bought the hotel - so they didn't have to keep dealing with those eating into profits. "We were at 8.5 per cent in those times - we got out of it for that reason," she said, adding that the financial pressures they faced in the hospitality industry were mainly thanks to power, insurance and labour costs. Despite the tightening financial screws, some industry veterans argue that focusing solely on interest rates misses the forest for the trees. Nutrien Western principal Boyd Curran, Longreach, suggests that the market's true north is still dictated by two traditional forces: weather and global supply. He points to a massive tailwind coming from across the Pacific, thanks to the US cattle herd being at its lowest level in decades. "I don't think at this stage the interest rates are having an impact," he said. "At the end of the day, it's supply and demand. The cattle and sheep meat markets are extremely strong." The value proposition in western Queensland was massive, he said, particularly for those converting traditional grazing land into value-added sorghum production. From this perspective, the current rate hikes are a hurdle, but not a barricade. If global demand remains record-breaking, the protein boom might just provide the margins necessary to outpace the bank's rising interest bills. "My thoughts on interest rates are typical, like everyone in the bush," Mr Curran said. "If this Labor government stops spending so recklessly, we'd be in a lot better spot. "I think that that is putting a lot of pressure on inflation. "Well, not a lot of pressure, I think it's driving inflation, and if they just pull their heads in, and be a bit more responsible, then we'd see inflation quickly come back under control and we'd see interest rates come down."

Secondary effects, as explained by USQ's Professor John Cole, include a stronger Australian dollar reducing export competitiveness and potential cuts to rural infrastructure spending. While some, like finance specialist Brecken Curtis and publican Tracy Hatch, highlight the tightening financial pressure and its ripple effects on rural communities, others like Nutrien's Boyd Curran argue that strong global demand driven by low US cattle supplies currently outweighs the impact of interest rates.

Headline: It's 'toes on turf' Saturday
Full Text:
For over 50 years, Jimboomba Turf has been Southeast Queensland's most trusted turf supplier, deeply rooted in Queensland soil and committed to growing the finest quality turf for homes, businesses and communities right across the state.

    Jimboomba Turf supplies premium varieties that thrive in our famous Queensland climate. Varieties such as Sir Walter DNA Certified Buffalo, Sir Grange Zoysia, TifTuf Bermuda and Zoysia Australis are grown right here in Southeast Queensland, producing a product that is a cut above the rest. Each variety comes with Aus
    GAP certification, which guarantees genetic purity and a weed, pest and disease-free lawn at the time of harvest. Grown with care and harvested fresh to ensure every lawn starts strong and stays lush for years to come, Jimboomba Turf are here to ensure you and your family always have a lawn you'll love.

    What truly sets them apart goes beyond the paddock. It is over five decades of horticultural knowledge, a company-owned delivery fleet, and genuine aftercare support, meaning customers are looked after from the moment they enquire, and well past the establishment of their new lawn.

    Jimboomba Turf is passionate about creating green spaces across our sunny state, because they know that memories are made in the backyard.

    Nothing brings family and friends together like a welcoming backyard oasis. Whether it's a barefoot Summer, or a mud-free Winter that you're chasing, instant turf is the fast affordable landscape solution that you've been searching for.

    For Jimboomba Turf, it's always been about more than lawns. It's about community, and this weekend you can be a part of it. Join the crew this Saturday, February 14, for a morning of good oldfashioned family fun!

    The event will take place from 9am to 12pm at Flagstone Water Play Park on Flagstonian Drive, Undullah.

    There'll be a free BBQ, face painting, a coffee van to keep the adults going, and a live cross from Rebel Radio, all wrapped up with the friendly hospitality that's been the Jimboomba way for half a century. Bring the family, meet your neighbours, and celebrate great lawns and great community.

    We can't wait to see you there!

Headline: Side-saddles advancing traditional skills
Full Text:
Australian Side Saddle enthusiasts at Versedale Scrub are planning a massive year, with the side saddle discipline increasing in popularity as interest in traditional horsemanship grows.

    The group's founder Simone Johnston and her mother Sandie proudly rode into the equestrian ring at the Stanthorpe Show, which ran from 30 January to 1 February.

    "We were thrilled to be approached by the Stanthorpe Show to run a special side saddle program as part of their horse competition at their special 150th event," Johnston said.

    "The Australian Side Saddle Community was more than happy to accommodate this request and proudly sponsor the program which boasted a five class program for competitors.

    Sandie Johnson made her debut ride in her brand new Syd Hill side saddle, an Australianmade saddle produced just up the road in Brisbane.

    "Syd Hill produced only a limited number of side saddles during the late 1970s and into the 1980s. As a result, these saddles are highly sought after and, due to their popularity and rarity, rarely come up for sale."

    "We were also invited to present a side saddle display for the public.

    "This living history demonstration attracted incredible interest from people of all ages and backgrounds, showcasing the elegance, tradition, and historical significance of side saddle riding."

    With growing interest from other show societies and a big event planned for later this year.

    Johnson said the first ever ASSC side saddle clinic on 27 June would feature side saddle rider, coach, and advocate, Maggie Pritchard McAllister from the USA.

    The ASSC side saddle clinic will be held at Bellara Park Equestrian Centre, a covered arena at Veresdale.

Mother and daughter Sandie and Simone Johnson at the Stanthorpe Show. (Hoofprintz Photography)

Headline: Airy home with view
Full Text:
CORNUBIA'S 24 Penelope Drive was designed and built to suit its sloped block and Queensland's year-round warm climate, owner Rose Blair said.

    "In summer, I rarely put an air conditioner on unless we have exceptionally horrible days," Ms Blair said.

    "But generally, it's very airy, and being up high means you can take advantage of the view."

    Ms Blair and her family designed the home in 1991, raised on poles or stilts, specifically to suit the year-round warm Queensland environment and facilitate the flow of cool air under the house Ms Blair said this was the second pole home she had built.

    "The first was in Newcastle, New South Wales, in the 80s when there were hardly any pole homes in Newcastle.

    "They originated in New Zealand, but they re everywhere here in Queensland.," Ms Blair didn't have any experience designing property before these two homes, but knew she wanted open plan living and a unique home.

    "We built the home to try and bring the outside in," Ms Blair said.

    To page 22 Stained glass windows add eclectic touch to pole home

    From page 19 "We've got beautiful, sweeping views and windows everywhere," Stained glass windows Ms Blair made herself, featuring koalas, gum leaves and lorikeets that lead onto the back verandah, add an eclectic touch to the home.

    "When we first moved in, back in the early 90s, there were koalas everywhere," Ms Blair said.

    "It was crazy, one night we got the spotlight out and counted 13 koalas "I just love putting my stamp on a property, and the only way to do that was to do my own thing with the windows, so I did a course on stained glass," Since the original build, Ms Blair has renovated the bathroom, re-finished the timber floors and touched up the interior paint work.

    But the home remains in pristine original condition.

    "It's going to be hard to find another place that I love as much," Ms Blair said.

    She said the property should attract interest from families or a young couple who would be well-suited to the space, stairs, outdoor living, and 2002sqm block.

    There is so much that can be done with this property, even underneath it," Ms Blair said.

    "There is so much that can be done with this property, even underneath it," Ms Blair said. "I've had plans drawn up to put a studio in.

    "The block could be subdivided to put two or three properties on it."

The owner built this home in 1991.

Headline: Make friends, connect and enjoy life with Probus Club
Full Text:
PROBUS clubs around the country are cele brating 50 years since the first club in Australia was formed on 3 February 1976 in Sydney.

    Logan Village's Probus Club is much younger, starting on 16 October 2024 and growing its membership to 120 in less than a year.

    Probus clubs are designed to connect older locals for regular social meetings and activities, Logan Village Probus Club president Cherylyn Hitchin said.

    "Our club provides a welcoming space where people can meet new friends, stay active and enjoy being part of their local community," she said.

    "Probus is about friendship, staying connected and giving people something to look forward to.

    "It's about having fun, catching up with friends and enjoying life together."

    Probus clubs are run by club members and tailored to suit local community interests.

    More than 90,000 Australians are part of 1300 Probus clubs.

    The Logan Village Probus Club meets at the Logan Village Community Hall on Wharf Street from 10am to 12pm on the third Wednesday of the month and is open to anyone retired or semi-retired.

    For more information, visit https: / /probussouthpacific.org/.

story_title: Logan Community Groups Foster Support and Connection
story_summary: Several community-focused initiatives are underway across Logan. A bright yellow community bench was unveiled at Boronia Bushland Reserve to raise awareness of domestic and family violence, in memory of Allison Baden-Clay. The City Women Logan forum, hosted by Impact Care Service, will feature keynote speaker Karen Muras, a domestic violence survivor who now builds tiny homes for women in need. For seniors, the new Logan Village Probus Club has seen rapid growth to 120 members since its start in October 2024. Additionally, the Logan Youth Action Group is recruiting young people aged 14-24 to get involved in local projects and workshops.

Headline: Drones, dogs, divers in search for woman
Full Text:
INVESTIGATIONS into the disappearance of Logan Central woman Dawn Wells continue as police on Wednesday searched areas of Slacks Creek and Underwood for information.

    Police also released video of Ms Wells hours before she was last seen at a bus stop on Kingston Road, Woodridge, at around 8pm on 9 December 2025.

    Acting Inspector Warren Parker said a police dog squad, dive squad and SES personnel with aerial drones helped in Wednesday's search for Ms Wells in and around the intersection of Compton and Kingston roads.

    "Dawn is actually 70 years of age," Acting Inspector Parker said.

    "She does have health vulnerabilities, which require constant medication, and we know from our extensive and intensive inquiries in relation to this matter that Dawn hasn't engaged with her regular appointments to have that medication.

    "We do have concerns around her health at the moment, and we think this may have led to her disappearance and the surrounding factors behind it."2 Acting Inspector Parker said there was nothing to indicate any suspicious activity in relation to her disappearance and police had followed up on information Ms Wells had been seen at Hervey Bay, Brisbane City, the Gold Coast, Sydney and Melbourne.

    "Every lead that comes through, we follow." He said police hoped to locate personal items Ms Wells was wearing or carrying on the day she disappeared and urged community members to come forward with information about her whereabouts.

    Ms Wells is described as Caucasian, about 160cm tall, with a slim petite build, shoulder-length brown curly hair, hazel eyes and walks with a distinctive gate.

    Phone Policelink with information on 131 444.

Headline: Young people urged to help
Full Text:
LOGAN Youth Action Group is calling all young people in Logan aged 14-24 to join their crew and help make a positive change in the community.

    The group is dedicated to empowering young people to act, inspire and create positive change, by getting them involved in community projects and activities, workshops, local council meetings and networking opportunities. To get involved, phone the community development team on 3412 3412.

story_title: Logan Community Groups Foster Support and Connection
story_summary: Several community-focused initiatives are underway across Logan. A bright yellow community bench was unveiled at Boronia Bushland Reserve to raise awareness of domestic and family violence, in memory of Allison Baden-Clay. The City Women Logan forum, hosted by Impact Care Service, will feature keynote speaker Karen Muras, a domestic violence survivor who now builds tiny homes for women in need. For seniors, the new Logan Village Probus Club has seen rapid growth to 120 members since its start in October 2024. Additionally, the Logan Youth Action Group is recruiting young people aged 14-24 to get involved in local projects and workshops.
